text: This is a robotic assisted laparoscopic interparietal hernia repair after kidney and liver transplantation. This patient was a fifty nine year old woman with past history of end stage liver disease complicated by hepatorenal syndrome who had undergone a simultaneous liver and kidney transplantation in twenty seventeen and later presented in twenty nineteen with a year long history of intermittent right lower quadrant abdominal pain. Physical exam revealed a palpable bulge in her right lower quadrant associated with her prior kidney transplant incision. Here is a representative image of the patient's CT scan that includes the transplanted kidney and no definitive evidence of hernia in the right lower quadrant.

While the patient's imaging was not conclusive for evidence of hernia, given her symptoms and the presence of a bulge on physical exam, it was decided that the patient should proceed with a diagnostic laparoscopy and possible robotic assisted laparoscopic abdominal wall reconstruction.

The surgeon achieves access to the abdomen using a 5mm optical entry trocar in the left upper quadrant, followed by placement of two 8mm robotic trocars in the left hemiabdomen. The entry port is upsized to an 8mm trocar. The video reveals a 7x6 cm intraparietal hernia extending through the transversus abdominis and internal oblique muscles, with the external oblique muscle remaining intact. Moderate lysis of adhesions is performed, bringing the abdominal wall defect into view in the right abdomen.

The surgeon creates a preperitoneal plane starting on the left side of the midline and extends it across the midline. On the right side, the posterior rectus sheath is incised to enter the retrorectus plane, which is then extended laterally. The lateral posterior rectus sheath is incised, re-entering the preperitoneal plane. Dissection reveals the abdominal wall defect and the intact external oblique muscle as the peritoneum is carefully separated and released from the area of weakness.

The surgeon dissects over the transplanted kidney to create lateral overlap for mesh placement. After achieving the overlap, the fascial defect is closed with a running number one Stratafix barbed suture. An eighteen by five centimeters soft barred mesh is inserted to reinforce the primary repair, secured with interrupted two O Vicryl sutures.

A 5mm trocar is inserted into the right upper quadrant to introduce a 19 French Blake drain into the extraperitoneal space adjacent to the mesh. The peritoneum is closed with a running 2-0 V-lock barbed suture, and the surgical drain is opened to the air to evacuate CO2 from the extraperitoneal space.

The patient had an uneventful post-operative course. She was advanced to a regular diet without incident and discharged on post-op day two with her drain still in place. At her post-operative visit on post-op day ten, she was doing very well, denied significant pain, and had returned to moderate activity.
